title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text: Wind resource modelling provides the probability distribution of wind states used to calculate annual production. The hydrogen configurations require more than mean wind speed, because stack efficiency and compressor operation depend on instantaneous power.

title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text: Wind resource modelling provides the probability distribution of wind states used to calculate annual production. The hydrogen configurations require more than mean wind speed, because stack efficiency and compressor operation depend on instantaneous power.

title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text:
Current Method
The background model describes fitting approximately ten years of offshore wind observations from the Dutch Offshore Wind Atlas to a Weibull distribution and wind rose.
For each wind state, the model evaluates turbine output and wake losses. The annual yield is then obtained by weighting the power output of each state by its probability.

title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text:
Energy Balance
The wind resource page does not apply conversion losses directly. It supplies gross turbine power states to downstream components:

electricity components apply electrical efficiency and availability factors
hydrogen production evaluates stack efficiency and balance-of-plant losses at each operating point
compression power depends on hydrogen flow and pressure ratio

title: Wind Resource and Weibull Distribution
text:
Source Status
The document references Dutch Offshore Wind Atlas data and a fitted wind rose. The exact data file and fitted Weibull parameters should be linked here once the model input dataset is finalised.

title: Wind Resource and Layout
text:
This section documents the wind resource, wake modelling, spacing, and infield-layout assumptions that determine gross energy production and infrastructure length.
The wind-resource and layout model connects the physical site to component sizing. Turbine positions determine wake losses and infield network geometry. Wake-aware production determines both annual yield and the operating points of hydrogen conversion equipment.

title: Wind Resource and Layout
text:
Main Dependencies

turbine count and sea area
turbine spacing and farm power density
wake model
infield cable or pipeline topology
export distance

title: Wind Turbine

title: Wind Turbine
text:
Energy Balance
The turbine power curve and wake model determine gross turbine output. For the electricity configuration, the turbine output measurement is treated as being after the inverter and before the internal transformer. The transformer is included in total turbine cost but its efficiency is accounted for in the electrical chain.
For decentralised hydrogen, the electrical conversion chain differs because the PEM stack can connect to the turbine DC link rather than using the full inverter-transformer path.

Mass and Installation
Turbine mass is not yet fully parameterised in the source material. Tower, nacelle, and component masses should be added where they affect tower cost, foundation loading, or installation vessel requirements.

title: Turbine System
text:
The turbine system includes the wind turbine, generator, turbine-side power electronics, tower and nacelle mass, and foundation.
The turbine system is common to all architectures at the level of aerodynamic energy capture, but the downstream electrical conversion chain changes between electricity, centralised hydrogen, and decentralised hydrogen.

title: Turbine System
text:
Current Baseline
The current model uses a 15 MW turbine as the baseline unit. Turbine and foundation assumptions are documented separately because foundations, tower mass, and installation costs can scale with mass and structural loading.

text: The foundation supports the offshore wind turbine and transfers turbine, tower, nacelle, and environmental loads to the seabed.

Mass and Scaling
The current foundation cost is fixed per 15 MW turbine. A future refinement should connect foundation cost to turbine rating, water depth, soil condition, tower/nacelle mass, and hydrogen equipment mass where relevant.

title: Scaling Laws
text:
Discrete Scaling
Some infrastructure is discrete rather than continuously scalable:

HVDC export capacity follows 2 GW block logic in the current source framing.
Export pipelines are selected from discrete diameter and pressure options.
Stack modules are 2.5 MW units.

Discrete scaling can create optima where slightly lower yield is accepted to avoid adding another large component.

LCOE Treatment
Annualised CAPEX, annual OPEX, and annual delivered energy are combined as:

This conversion convention should be made explicit for every imported cost source.

title: Platforms
text:
Platforms host offshore aggregation, conversion, and hydrogen production equipment. They affect CAPEX, installation cost, structural mass, and architecture feasibility.
The model currently uses a high-level platform cost basis from the HVDC/substation package and reuses the platform-cost assumption for centralised electrolysis where a separate platform model is not yet available.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
The model boundary runs from wind turbine generation to the onshore delivery point. It includes turbines, foundations, infield collection, offshore platforms or substations, conversion equipment, export infrastructure, and onshore conversion or delivery interface.
This boundary is used because offshore electricity and offshore hydrogen differ mainly in the infrastructure required between the turbine and the shore. Excluding export infrastructure would make the comparison structurally biased toward electricity, because the electrical pathway would be evaluated before its largest carrier-specific cost items enter the calculation.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
The model boundary runs from wind turbine generation to the onshore delivery point. It includes turbines, foundations, infield collection, offshore platforms or substations, conversion equipment, export infrastructure, and onshore conversion or delivery interface.
This boundary is used because offshore electricity and offshore hydrogen differ mainly in the infrastructure required between the turbine and the shore. Excluding export infrastructure would make the comparison structurally biased toward electricity, because the electrical pathway would be evaluated before its largest carrier-specific cost items enter the calculation.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
LCOE Definition
The levelised cost of energy is calculated as annualised lifetime cost divided by annual delivered energy:
LCOE = annualised_cost / annual_energy_delivered
For electricity configurations, delivered energy is electrical energy at the onshore grid connection point. For hydrogen configurations, hydrogen yield is converted to energy using the higher heating value.

title: System Boundary and LCOE
text:
Conceptual Cost Structure
The model uses the following conceptual decomposition to explain why infrastructure affects optimal design:
LCOE = C1 / Yield + C2 / Capacity Factor + C3
where:

C1 represents fixed project costs that do not scale strongly with installed capacity or yield.
C2 represents capacity-linked infrastructure costs, such as turbines, foundations, converters, platforms, cables, electrolysers, compressors, and pipelines.
C3 represents energy-linked costs or losses, including conversion penalties and variable operating costs.

This is a conceptual expression, not the full implementation. Its role is to show why a high cost per MW of infrastructure penalises low-capacity-factor designs: infrastructure is sized for peak power, while revenue and LCOE recovery depend on delivered annual energy.

title: Optimisation Variables
text: The model treats several design choices as optimisation variables rather than fixed assumptions. Their values are outcomes of the system cost minimisation.

title: Optimisation Variables
text:
Layout Variables
Turbine spacing and wind farm power density determine the trade-off between areal yield and capacity factor. Tighter spacing increases installed capacity per area but increases wake losses. Wider spacing improves capacity factor but lowers MWh/km2.

title: Optimisation Variables
text:
Hydrogen Variables
For hydrogen configurations, the main optimisation variables are:

stack overplant factor: installed electrolyser capacity relative to turbine or farm rated power
pipeline operating pressure
pipeline diameter or selected pipeline class
number of export pipelines, where pipeline capacity is discrete

The stack overplant factor affects both conversion efficiency and CAPEX. Oversizing the stack lowers current density and can improve efficiency, but increases stack CAPEX.
Pipeline pressure and diameter affect both transport capacity and compression energy. Because pipelines are discrete assets, the cost optimum may occur by accepting a small energy-yield loss if it avoids an additional pipeline.

title: Optimisation Variables
text:
Electricity Variables
For the electricity configuration, export capacity is constrained by HVDC platform and cable block sizing. The current model assumes a 2 GW export cable/platform basis, which creates discrete capacity increments rather than a continuously scalable export link.

title: Optimisation Variables
text:
Architecture-Specific Feasible Sets
Each architecture has its own feasible set:

electricity export requires AC infield collection, offshore conversion, HVDC export, and onshore conversion
centralised hydrogen keeps the AC infield network but replaces HVDC export with offshore electrolysis, compression, and hydrogen pipeline export
decentralised hydrogen moves electrolysis into the turbine and replaces the infield AC network with a hydrogen pipeline grid

title: Energy, Availability, and Annualisation
text:
Efficiency Treatment
Efficiency losses are applied at the component level. For electricity, this includes transformer, infield AC grid, offshore substation, HVDC export cable, and onshore conversion losses. For hydrogen, losses include stack conversion, balance-of-plant auxiliary demand, purification losses, power electronics where present, and compression.

title: Hydrogen Production
text:
Hydrogen production covers the entire electrolyser. In this work, always a PEM electrolyser is studied.
The electrolyser is split up in the following components: * stack * power electronics * balance of plant (BOP) Note that in most contexts, the balance of plants includes the power electronics, but here they are explictly treated separately.
The compressor is not included in this section, but is under Hydrogen Infrastructure: Compressor
The hydrogen production model is dynamic: stack efficiency and auxiliary power consumption depend on operating point, which depends on the wind state and the installed stack capacity.

title: Hydrogen Infrastructure
text:
Hydrogen infrastructure includes compression, infield hydrogen pipelines, export pipelines, pressure selection, diameter selection, and pipeline capacity.
Compression and pipeline assumptions are coupled: higher pipeline inlet pressure increases compression energy and compressor cost, but also increases pipeline capacity and can reduce the number of parallel export pipelines.
The current hydrogen infrastructure model assumes no additional hydrogen losses in pipelines after compression.

title: Compressor
text:
The compressor raises hydrogen from electrolyser outlet pressure to the required pipeline inlet pressure. It is present in centralised and decentralised hydrogen architectures.
PEM stack outlet pressure is assumed to be about 30 bar. Pipeline inlet pressure is an optimisation variable, with examples up to 150 bar.

title: Compressor
text:

PEM stack outlet pressure is assumed to be about 30 bar. Pipeline inlet pressure is an optimisation variable, with examples up to 150 bar.

title: Electricity Export with HVDC
text:
Modelling Implications
The key modelling feature is that export infrastructure is sized for peak installed capacity. HVDC platforms and export cables therefore contribute strongly to the capacity-linked cost term.
The current assumption set uses a 2 GW HVDC export basis. This creates discrete export capacity increments and makes under-utilisation of peak-sized infrastructure important in the LCOE calculation.

title: About
text:
This site documents the assumptions behind a component-level techno-economic model for offshore wind infrastructure.
The model compares electricity-based export, centralised offshore hydrogen production, and decentralised turbine-integrated hydrogen production. The documentation here is limited to methodology and assumptions: system boundaries, component cost models, energy balances, availability factors, installation assumptions, and shared financial conventions.
The source material is a set of working Word documents in background/. Some assumptions rely on internal supplier or manufacturer data; those are documented at the level needed for model reproducibility without exposing confidential raw datasets.

title: Centralised Hydrogen
text: The centralised hydrogen architecture keeps the conventional wind turbine and AC infield collection system up to the offshore aggregation point. At the offshore platform, electricity is converted to hydrogen using electrolysers, compressed, and exported through a subsea hydrogen pipeline.

title: Centralised Hydrogen

title: Centralised Hydrogen
text:
Modelling Implications
This architecture replaces the HVDC converter platform and HVDC export cable with a central electrolyser, compressor, and hydrogen pipeline. It retains many electrical infield assumptions, but changes the export infrastructure and introduces conversion losses.
The central facility can benefit from economies of scale in the non-electrical balance of plant. Stack and power electronics costs are treated as modular and scale approximately linearly.

title: Decentralised Hydrogen
text: The decentralised architecture places electrolysis inside the turbine. Energy is converted to hydrogen as close as possible to generation, and the infield network becomes a hydrogen pipeline grid instead of an AC cable grid.

title: Decentralised Hydrogen
text: The decentralised architecture places electrolysis inside the turbine. Energy is converted to hydrogen as close as possible to generation, and the infield network becomes a hydrogen pipeline grid instead of an AC cable grid.

title: Decentralised Hydrogen
text:
Component Chain

onshore hydrogen backbone connection

title: Decentralised Hydrogen
text:
Modelling Implications
This architecture removes several electrical conversion steps. In the centralised and electricity configurations, turbine output is converted from variable-frequency AC to DC, then to grid-quality AC, then stepped up for infield collection. For central electrolysis, the power is later stepped down and rectified again before entering the stack.
In the decentralised configuration, the stack connects more directly to the turbine DC link. The model therefore excludes the turbine inverter/step-up transformer and the platform-side step-down transformer/rectifier that would otherwise be needed for electrolysis.
The infield grid is also structurally different. A 66 kV AC string is capacity-limited to roughly 60-70 MW, while a 6 inch hydrogen pipeline at 150 bar is assumed to carry roughly 500 MW equivalent. This removes the electrical string capacity constraint from the infield design.

title: Architectures
text:
The model compares three offshore wind export architectures:
The architectures differ in where energy conversion occurs and which infrastructure carries energy to shore.

title: Architectures
text:
Architecture Logic
The electrical architecture transports electrons to shore. The hydrogen architectures convert wind power to hydrogen offshore and transport gas to shore. The centralised hydrogen architecture converts at an offshore platform. The decentralised hydrogen architecture converts inside each turbine and uses an infield hydrogen grid.
The model treats these architectures as different feasible sets. Components are not swapped one-for-one; changing the carrier changes the cost, loss, availability, and installation structure of the whole value chain.

title: Pipeline Pressure and Capacity
text:
Optimisation Logic
Higher inlet pressure increases the capacity of a given pipeline but increases compression work. Larger diameters reduce cost per unit capacity but are limited by installation and flexibility constraints.
The source material notes that the cost optimum often prefers the largest feasible diameter because it has the lowest cost per capacity. However, because pipelines come in discrete units, they can be over-dimensioned. Reducing pressure can then lower compression cost while retaining enough capacity.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
The PEM electrolyser stack is the primary energy conversion component of the hydrogen production system. It converts electrical power and water into hydrogen and oxygen and therefore largely determines the overall efficiency of the electrolyser.
Within this model, the stack is represented explicitly because its efficiency, degradation and sizing have a strong influence on the optimal design of the offshore energy system. Rather than optimising the stack in isolation, it is evaluated as one component of the complete offshore hydrogen production chain.
PEM stacks are present in both centralised and decentralised hydrogen production architectures.






NoteWhy PEM?



PEM electrolysis is selected because it is well suited to variable renewable electricity. The technology can rapidly follow fluctuations in wind power while maintaining high efficiency over a wide operating range.
Another important advantage is that hydrogen is produced at an elevated outlet pressure (approximately 30 bar in the source material), reducing the required downstream compression ratio compared with low-pressure electrolysis technologies.
See the Compressor page for more discussion.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
The PEM electrolyser stack is the primary energy conversion component of the hydrogen production system. It converts electrical power and water into hydrogen and oxygen and therefore largely determines the overall efficiency of the electrolyser.
Within this model, the stack is represented explicitly because its efficiency, degradation and sizing have a strong influence on the optimal design of the offshore energy system. Rather than optimising the stack in isolation, it is evaluated as one component of the complete offshore hydrogen production chain.
PEM stacks are present in both centralised and decentralised hydrogen production architectures.






NoteWhy PEM?



PEM electrolysis is selected because it is well suited to variable renewable electricity. The technology can rapidly follow fluctuations in wind power while maintaining high efficiency over a wide operating range.
Another important advantage is that hydrogen is produced at an elevated outlet pressure (approximately 30 bar in the source material), reducing the required downstream compression ratio compared with low-pressure electrolysis technologies.
See the Compressor page for more discussion.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Cost Model
The stack cost consists primarily of materials and manufacturing. Unlike equipment such as turbines or compressors, larger PEM electrolysers are not constructed by scaling individual stacks. Instead, larger systems simply contain more identical stack modules. Consequently, stack cost scales approximately linearly with installed stack capacity. Cost reductions are therefore expected to arise mainly through manufacturing learning and production volume rather than economies of scale at the equipment level. The model adopts the stack cost projections from .

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Energy Balance
Stack efficiency is calculated from a manufacturer polarisation curve relating cell voltage to current density. The operating point is defined as a percentage of maximum stack power or maximum current density.
Oversizing the stack lowers current density for a given power input and can improve efficiency, but increases CAPEX. The model therefore treats stack overplanting as an optimisation variable.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Module Switching
Because the electrolyser is modular, not every stack module has to operate simultaneously.
For every operating point, the model selects the number of active modules that maximises the overall conversion efficiency. The available electrical power is then distributed across the active modules.
This operational flexibility also alleviates the minimum-load limitation of PEM stacks, which is typically around 10% of the rated module capacity.
For example, a 10 MW electrolyser consisting of ten 1 MW modules can operate at only one active module and 10% load. The minimum system load therefore becomes approximately 100 kW rather than 1 MW, allowing hydrogen production to continue during periods of low wind generation without operating individual stacks below their safe operating limit.
Module switching is assumed to occur without efficiency or lifetime penalties.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Availability
The modular architecture provides inherent redundancy because individual stack modules can be isolated without shutting down the complete electrolyser. A stack availability of 99.5% is assumed throughout this study.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Installation and Mass
Stack installation is included within the installation model of the complete electrolyser facility or turbine-integrated hydrogen production system.
A dedicated stack mass model is not yet included. If future work identifies stack mass as a significant driver for tower sizing, platform design or installation costs, this section should be expanded accordingly.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Source Basis
Stack cost assumptions are based on NREL-derived cost data and manufacturer efficiency data. The manufacturer polarisation curve is treated as confidential source data.

This encyclopedia documents the methodology and techno-economic assumptions used to model offshore wind infrastructure, electricity export, offshore hydrogen production, and hydrogen transport.
The site is organised as an assumptions manual. Each component page bundles the modelling choices that matter for that component:
The emphasis is deliberately methodological. Results, scenario outcomes, and interpretation of model output are out of scope for this encyclopedia.

title: Methodology
text:
This section defines the modelling logic used across all component pages. The model is a deterministic techno-economic optimisation framework for offshore wind systems with alternative energy carriers.
The modelling object is the complete turbine-to-onshore-delivery value chain. The purpose is not to compare isolated component prices, but to represent how component cost, energy conversion, availability, and installation assumptions interact when the energy carrier changes from electricity to hydrogen.

title: Methodology
text:
Methodological Role
The methodology pages define conventions that are shared across components:

the system boundary used for cost and yield accounting
the LCOE objective function
the modelling workflow from fixed inputs to derived parameters and outputs
the optimisation variables treated as model outcomes
the annualisation, energy-basis, and availability conventions used in component calculations

Component-specific assumptions are not collected here. They are documented on the relevant component pages.

title: Methodology
text:
Core Principle
Infrastructure is modelled as part of the offshore energy system, not as an external connection. This matters because export infrastructure is sized for peak capacity but paid back through delivered annual energy. The cost of capacity therefore changes the economically optimal layout and carrier choice.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text: The generator produces variable-frequency AC power. Power electronics condition this output for either AC grid injection or electrolysis.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text: The generator produces variable-frequency AC power. Power electronics condition this output for either AC grid injection or electrolysis.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text:
Electricity and Centralised Hydrogen Chain
For conventional electrical operation, the turbine output is rectified to DC, inverted to grid-quality AC, and stepped up by a transformer to the infield grid voltage.
Centralised hydrogen uses the same turbine-side chain up to the infield AC network. At the offshore platform, power is stepped down and rectified again before entering the electrolyser stacks.

title: Generator and Power Electronics
text:
Decentralised Hydrogen Chain
In the decentralised architecture, electrolysers are placed inside or at the turbine and can connect to the turbine DC link. The inverter and step-up transformer on the turbine side are not required for the hydrogen pathway, and the platform-side step-down transformer and rectifier are absent.

Until explicit mass functions are added, turbine CAPEX, foundation CAPEX, and installation cost are represented through fixed component assumptions. Any future mass-linked correction should state:

mass driver
affected component cost
scaling relation
source basis
architecture applicability

title: Infield Layout
text: Infield layout determines the length and topology of the collection network between turbines and the offshore aggregation or export point. The model distinguishes between electrical cable strings and hydrogen pipeline ladders.

title: Infield Layout
text: Infield layout determines the length and topology of the collection network between turbines and the offshore aggregation or export point. The model distinguishes between electrical cable strings and hydrogen pipeline ladders.

title: Infield Layout
text:
Electrical Infield Layout
The electrical infield network is constrained by cable capacity. The current assumption set uses a 66 kV AC infield cable capacity of 70 MW. Large farms therefore require multiple strings, each connecting only a limited number of turbines.
The model estimates average string length from farm geometry rather than solving a detailed cable-routing optimisation. The average string length is approximated from the distance between the farm centre and corner. An additional 20% is added to represent diversions and crossing constraints.

title: Infield Layout
text:
Hydrogen Infield Layout
The hydrogen infield network is not constrained in the same way. The current assumption assumes 6 inch infield pipelines at 150 bar with about 500 MW equivalent capacity. This enables a ladder topology where each turbine has two possible routes to the export pipeline.
For a square layout, the ladder length is estimated from the side length of the farm and the number of rungs, which scales with the square root of turbine count.

title: Infield Layout
text:
Cost and Installation Link
Infield layout feeds directly into component cost pages:

Infield AC Cables
Hydrogen Pipelines
Cable Installation
Pipeline Installation

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text: Wake modelling translates turbine layout into gross and wake-corrected energy yield. Turbine spacing is the main design variable connecting energy production to infrastructure economics.

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text: Wake modelling translates turbine layout into gross and wake-corrected energy yield. Turbine spacing is the main design variable connecting energy production to infrastructure economics.

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text:
Current Method
The model uses PyWake to construct a wake-aware power lookup table indexed by wind speed and direction. Turbine positions are arranged on a rectangular grid scaled to the sea area and turbine count.

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text:
Yield-Capacity Factor Trade-off
Tighter spacing increases installed capacity per km2 and can raise total areal yield. It also increases wake losses and lowers the capacity factor. Wider spacing reduces wake losses and improves capacity factor, but uses more area per MW.
This trade-off is architecture-dependent because capacity-linked infrastructure costs differ across electricity and hydrogen configurations.

title: Wake Modelling and Spacing
text:
Cost Link
Spacing affects:

number of turbines for a fixed area and density
wake losses and annual energy yield
capacity factor
infield cable or pipeline length
utilisation of peak-sized export infrastructure

The model therefore treats spacing or wind farm power density as a design variable, not merely a site descriptor.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Polarisation Curve
Engineering principle
The electrical energy required to produce hydrogen depends on the cell voltage. As current density increases, the cell voltage rises because activation, ohmic and mass transport losses become larger. Consequently, stack efficiency decreases at higher current densities.
Model implementation
Stack efficiency is calculated from a manufacturer polarisation curve relating cell voltage to current density. The operating point is defined as a percentage of the maximum current density (equivalently the maximum stack power).








Figure 1: Manufacturer polarisation curve (top) and resulting stack efficiency (bottom) used in the model.





The optimisation therefore captures the trade-off between operating fewer stacks at high current density or more stacks at lower current density.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Modularity
Commercial PEM electrolysers are typically manufactured as modular units with capacities of approximately 1 MW. Larger electrolysers are constructed by combining many identical modules.
The modular approach offers several practical advantages:

repeatable manufacturing
manageable electrical voltages and currents
improved thermal management
easier transport and installation
simplified maintenance and replacement

The model therefore represents an electrolyser as a collection of identical stack modules.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Oversizing
The above reasoning can be extended to considering more stacks. Oversizing the stack modules lowers the current density per stack for a given power input and can improve efficiency. This comes at the cost of more material and CAPEX, but can be offset by the higher efficiency. The model therefore treats stack overplanting as a decision or optimisation variable, where the optimal stack sizing the LCOE.
The more components that are considered in the LCOE, the more this optimum tends towards higher efficiency over lower capex. See the text box below for an example.
Additionally, considering availability/redundancy and stack degradation, stack oversizing is even more attractive.






NoteExample: stack oversizing, and why the scope matters



When evaluating an electrolyser (power electronics, stack, BOP) only, on costs and performance. In that system, the stack makes up a significant share of the costs, let’s say 30% of the total costs. Increasing the stack size by maybe 50% will only be feasible when resulting efficiency gain will rise by 50%*30%=15%, which seems rather undoable.
Now consider evaluating the total offshore wind system, so not only the electrolyser, but the turbines, infrastructure and installation costs also. In that case, the stack material costs is only a small part, probably not more than 5%. A similar 50% increase is stack size is then beneficial, when the efficiency gain is only 50%*5%=2.5%, which is much more likely.
This underlines the importance of evaluating the offshore energy system holistically, and not optimising the individual components.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Degradation
Efficiency loss
PEM stacks gradually degrade during operation. As degradation progresses, the cell voltage increases, reducing the electrical efficiency of hydrogen production. The model assumes linear degradation with accumulated full-load operating hours at a rate of 0.18% per 1000 full-load hours.
Stack replacement
Eventually the efficiency loss becomes sufficiently large that replacing the stack is economically preferable to continued operation. Rather than prescribing a fixed replacement criterion, the replacement moment is determined through the LCOH optimisation. In practice, approximately 10% performance degradation is commonly considered end-of-life.
Effect of partial-load operation
Operating more installed stack capacity reduces the average current density of each module, slowing degradation and extending stack lifetime. This creates an additional economic incentive for stack overplanting, alongside the efficiency improvements described previously.






NoteExample: Stack overplanting and degradation



Assume a stack is replaced after reaching 10% degradation. With a degradation rate of 0.18% per 1000 full-load hours, replacement occurs after approximately 58,500 full-load hours. Assuming an offshore wind capacity factor of 40%, this corresponds to roughly 16.7 calendar years. A 25-year project therefore requires at least one stack replacement. If this replacement is already unavoidable, installing additional stack capacity from the outset becomes an attractive alternative. The total stack material requirement over the project lifetime remains similar, while the system additionally benefits from:

Lower maintenance costs by avoiding mid-life replacement.
Higher efficiency through operation at lower current density.
Reduced degradation throughout the project lifetime.

This conclusion depends strongly on the assumption that degradation scales linearly with accumulated full-load hours.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Stack Model
The stack model consists of four interacting components:

stack efficiency
modular operation
degradation
cost

Together these determine the optimal installed stack capacity and operating strategy.

title: PEM Electrolyser Stack
text:
Stack Overplanting
The modular architecture naturally allows installation of more stack capacity than the maximum available electrical input. This is referred to here as stack overplanting.
Operating a larger installed stack capacity reduces the average current density of each active module. Lower current density increases conversion efficiency and slows stack degradation.
These benefits come at the expense of additional stack CAPEX. The model therefore treats the installed stack capacity as an optimisation variable and determines the economically optimal degree of overplanting by minimising the overall LCOE.
In this LCOE optimisation, the system scope greatly influences the outcome. As the scope expands, the optimum generally shifts towards larger stack capacities and higher efficiencies, as stack costs become a smaller fraction of the total system investment. See the box below for a worked example.






NoteExample: Why the optimisation scope matters



Consider first an electrolyser system consisting only of the power electronics, stacks and balance of plant.
If the stack represents roughly 30% of the total electrolyser cost, increasing the installed stack capacity by 50% increases the overall system CAPEX by approximately 15%. Such an investment is only justified when the resulting efficiency improvement produces comparable lifetime savings.
Now consider the complete offshore hydrogen production system, including wind turbines, electrical infrastructure, offshore installation and export systems.
Within this larger system the stack may represent only 5% of the total investment A 50% increase in installed stack capacity therefore increases total project cost by only around 2.5%, making relatively small efficiency improvements economically worthwhile.
This illustrates why component optimisation should always be performed within the context of the complete energy system rather than considering each component independently.

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
The model assumes that, in a decentralised hydrogen architecture, the PEM electrolyser can be integrated directly on the wind turbine DC link. In other words, electrical power from the turbine is not exported as grid-quality AC and then rectified again for electrolysis. Instead, the turbine-side converter produces a controlled DC link, and the PEM stack or stack array is connected to that DC system.
The purpose of this section is to explain why that assumption is reasonable. The argument is made in two steps:

direct DC integration has a clear system-level benefit; and
direct DC integration without a separate electrolyser-side DC/DC converter is technically plausible if the turbine converter, PEM stack, and balance of plant are designed as one system.

The assumed electrical chain is:
wind generator -> active front-end rectifier / turbine-side converter -> controlled DC link -> PEM stack or PEM stack array
The turbine-side rectifier is assumed to be an active front end (AFE). It controls generator torque and DC-link voltage. The PEM stack or PEM stack array is connected directly to this controlled DC bus.
The architecture considered here has:

no AC grid export from the turbine;
no grid-side inverter;
no separate electrolyser-side DC/DC converter;
no dedicated stack current regulator between the DC link and the PEM stack;
only protection and auxiliary devices such as DC-link capacitance, contactors, precharge circuits, dump load, brake chopper, crowbar, and emergency shutdown hardware.







NoteModel assumption



The model assumes that direct DC integration is possible at system level. This does not imply that a PEM stack can simply be connected to an uncontrolled DC source. It assumes an integrated design in which the turbine AFE, DC-link control, PEM stack configuration, module switching, and balance-of-plant controls jointly keep the stack within its allowable operating envelope.

title: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
Main conclusion
Direct DC coupling of a PEM electrolyser stack to a wind-turbine DC link without a DC/DC converter is technically feasible, provided the turbine converter and PEM stack are designed as a single integrated system.
The key condition is that the active front end must replace most of the functions normally performed by the electrolyser power supply. In practice, this means the turbine-side converter must regulate the DC-link voltage and generator torque so that the PEM stack current remains within its allowed operating envelope.
This is not a conventional commercial architecture today. Public evidence supports the concept, but there is limited public proof of MW-scale field operation where a PEM stack is directly connected to a wind-turbine DC link with no intermediate DC/DC converter.






NoteFeasibility verdict



Technically feasible, but not plug-and-play. The most credible version is an AFE-controlled turbine DC bus feeding a voltage-matched, segmented PEM stack array, with module switching and emergency dump/brake protection.
crumbs: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
Electrical feasibility
A PEM electrolyser stack is fundamentally a DC load. It does not require AC power. If the DC-link voltage is matched to the PEM stack voltage range, the stack can draw current directly from the DC bus.
The operating point is determined by the intersection of:

the controlled DC-link voltage from the turbine converter; and
the PEM stack polarization curve.

The stack voltage can be represented conceptually as:
V_stack = N_cells * V_cell(j, temperature, pressure, hydration, ageing)
where j is current density.
With no DC/DC converter, the stack current is not independently regulated by an electrolyser power supply. Instead, current is controlled indirectly by controlling the DC-link voltage. This is acceptable in principle because the assumed AFE can actively regulate the DC link.
The design therefore becomes a voltage-matched system:
turbine DC-link operating range ~= PEM stack operating voltage range
If that matching is done properly, direct connection is feasible.
crumbs: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
section: What the active front end must do
crumbs: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
Evidence from references

ITM Power and Ørsted wind-turbine electrolyser concept
ITM Power explicitly described a concept in which an electrolyser is placed at or near the turbine and connected to the turbine DC link:

“an electrolyser placed at the wind turbine … directly electrically connected to the DC link in the wind turbine”

ITM also stated:

“‘Type IV’ wind turbine generators and their ‘DC link’ have the potential to power the electrolyser directly”

and described the benefit as:

“fewer power conversion steps”

Source: ITM Power, Wind turbine electrolyser integration: https://itm-power.com/news/wind-turbine-electrolyser-integraion
This is directly relevant because it supports the exact direction considered here: using the turbine DC link to feed electrolysis rather than exporting AC power.
However, ITM also states:

“Such a system has not been demonstrated in practice”

Therefore, this source supports the feasibility and industrial relevance of direct DC-link coupling, but it does not prove a realised no-DC/DC MW system.
Evidence level: industrial concept; direct DC-link coupling explicitly proposed; no public proof of field demonstration.


Jing et al. non-grid-connected wind-hydrogen system
Jing et al. describe a non-grid-connected wind-hydrogen architecture that:

“directly connects the Source wind turbines and the Load hydrogen production devices through a DC bus”

They describe the electrolyser load as:

“a modular proton exchange membrane (PEM) water electrolytic hydrogen production array”

and state that the control strategy regulates:

“the size of the PEM array … and DC voltage collaboratively”

Source: Jing et al., Research on the Structure and Control Strategy of Energy Router for Non-grid-connected Source-Load Hydrogen Production System: https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s42835-021-00774-w.pdf
This is highly relevant because it shows the correct no-DC/DC control idea: use a controlled DC bus plus a modular PEM array, rather than placing a DC/DC converter between the DC bus and the stack.
This source proves the architecture as an academic control concept and simulation model. It does not prove commercial hardware.
Evidence level: academic concept / simulation; strong support for direct DC bus plus modular PEM array.


RMIT / Air 403 direct-coupled wind-to-PEM work
The RMIT work is important because it explicitly investigated direct wind-to-PEM coupling:

“without the need for intervening voltage converters and maximum power point tracking electronics”

Source: RMIT, Direct coupling of a fixed-pitch variable speed aerogenerator to PEM electrolysers in a wind-hydrogen system for remote area power supply: https://research-repository.rmit.edu.au/articles/conference_contribution/Direct_coupling_of_a_fixed-pitch_variable_speed_aerogenerator_to_PEM_electrolysers_in_a_wind-hydrogen_system_for_remote_area_power_supply/27369558
This is one of the clearest references showing that direct coupling without voltage conversion can physically work. However, it concerns a small aerogenerator / remote-area system, not a modern MW wind turbine with a controlled Type-IV DC link.
Evidence level: small-scale direct-coupling experiment / demonstration; physically relevant but not MW-scale proof.


DC wind turbine concepts usually retain DC/DC
A recent DC wind-turbine architecture paper notes that DC coupling is attractive because:

“wind turbine converters and electrolyzers operate internally with dc voltage”

and that DC approaches can reduce conversion equipment because:

“the number of conversion stages and filters is reduced”

However, the same paper states:

“A key component for the application of dc technology in WTs is a high-power dc-dc converter”

and identifies functions such as:

“stepping up/down dc voltages, galvanic isolation and fault ride-through capability”

Source: Evaluation of wind turbine concepts for coupling wind energy and electrolysis: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10010-025-00796-0
This is the strongest counterpoint. It shows that although direct DC coupling is attractive, mainstream DC wind-turbine concepts usually keep a DC/DC converter for voltage adaptation, isolation, and protection. Therefore, a no-DC/DC architecture is feasible but non-standard.
Evidence level: contemporary DC-wind architecture reference; supports DC coupling but indicates DC/DC remains the mainstream engineering solution.
crumbs: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
PEM-specific feasibility constraints

Polarization curve
A PEM stack has a nonlinear voltage-current characteristic. Around normal operation, small voltage changes can create large current changes. Without a DC/DC converter, the AFE must prevent the DC-link voltage from driving excessive current.
This is manageable if the PEM stack voltage window is intentionally matched to the DC-link voltage window.


Current-density limits
The PEM stack must remain below its maximum current density. Excessive current density increases heat generation, degradation, water demand, and gas-management stress.
A review of dynamic PEM operation notes:

“Dynamic operation of PEM electrolyzers via modulation of the operating current density brings with it several challenges - namely, heat management, gas crossover and associated safety concerns, and stack degradation.”

Source: https://arxiv.org/html/2405.06766v1
Direct DC coupling is therefore feasible only if current density is actively constrained by DC-link voltage control, wind curtailment, or stack-module switching.


Minimum load and gas crossover
Low-load operation can be a safety issue because hydrogen crossover becomes more significant relative to oxygen production. A direct wind-coupled system will naturally experience low-load and transient operation unless it is deliberately curtailed or shut down.
A 2025 RSC paper on PEM operation at differential pressure states that hydrogen permeation understanding is:

“critical towards developing effective gas recombination strategies that enable safe operation”

Source: https://pubs.rsc.org/en/Content/ArticleLanding/2025/EE/D5EE00048C
This matters because direct coupling must not simply allow the stack to idle at arbitrary low current and high pressure. The control system needs defined safe operating regions, purge logic, and shutdown thresholds.


Dynamic response
PEM electrolysers are suitable for dynamic renewable operation, but the stack and balance of plant still need controlled operating envelopes. A review of PEM dynamic operation states that renewable intermittency:

“poses several technological challenges and financial questions”

Source: https://www.mdpi.com/1996-1073/18/9/2154
Direct DC coupling increases the importance of dynamic qualification because the PEM stack is exposed more directly to wind-driven electrical dynamics.


Thermal and water management
At high current density, the stack produces more heat and requires adequate water flow and cooling. Without a DC/DC converter, the AFE and PEM balance of plant must coordinate closely to avoid energising the stack before water flow, temperature, pressure, and conductivity are in range.


Fault response
The most critical system event is a PEM stack trip while the turbine is generating. Without a DC/DC converter, there is less electrical decoupling. The turbine DC link can overvoltage quickly unless excess power is removed.
A credible no-DC/DC system therefore needs:

fast torque reduction by the AFE;
pitch or aerodynamic curtailment;
DC brake chopper or dump load;
high-speed stack isolation;
controlled DC-link discharge;
safe hydrogen shutdown sequence.

title: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
section: Comparison with using a DC/DC converter
text:
Comparison with using a DC/DC converter
A dedicated DC/DC converter gives direct stack current control, voltage matching, protection, and electrical decoupling. Removing it reduces conversion stages and may reduce cost, footprint, and losses, but it also removes the normal actuator used to control the PEM stack.








Architecture
Strength
Weakness




AC-coupled wind plus electrolyser rectifier
Standard equipment; strong stack-current control
Multiple conversion stages; larger electrical infrastructure


Wind DC bus plus dedicated DC/DC to PEM
Good stack control; voltage matching; protection
Additional conversion stage, cost, losses, and equipment


Wind DC bus plus integrated multiport converter
Flexible integration of stack, storage, dump load, auxiliaries
Complex converter design and controls


Battery or supercapacitor plus PEM DC bus
Smooths wind variability; protects stack
Adds cost, losses, controls, and maintenance


Direct DC link to PEM stack array, no DC/DC
Fewest power conversion stages; attractive for turbine-integrated electrolysis
Requires tight turbine-stack co-design; less standard; higher integration risk
crumbs: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
Assessment of direct DC connection

What is proven
Small-scale direct wind-to-PEM coupling without voltage converters has been studied and experimentally investigated. Academic models also support direct DC bus connection with modular PEM array switching.


What is not proven publicly
There is no clear public evidence of a modern MW-scale wind turbine directly feeding PEM stack terminals from its internal DC link without a DC/DC converter.


Practical conclusion
Direct connection of a PEM stack or PEM stack array to a wind-turbine DC link without a DC/DC converter is technically feasible if the turbine-side AFE is designed to regulate the DC link and protect the stack.
The best design is not a passive direct connection. It is an actively controlled direct connection:
AFE-controlled DC bus + voltage-matched PEM stack array + stack-module switching + emergency dump/brake protection
The concept is supported by industrial proposals and academic work, especially the ITM/Ørsted DC-link concept, Jing et al.’s modular PEM-array DC-bus system, and RMIT’s small-scale direct-coupling work. However, public evidence does not yet show a clear MW-scale field project proving direct PEM stack coupling to a wind-turbine DC link with no DC/DC converter.






ImportantFinal feasibility statement



Yes, direct DC coupling without an electrolyser-side DC/DC converter is technically feasible. It is not yet publicly proven as a standard MW-scale architecture. It should be treated as a turbine-electrolyser co-design problem in which the AFE, segmented PEM stack array, protection chopper, and balance-of-plant controls jointly replace the missing DC/DC converter.
crumbs: Direct DC Coupling of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
The model assumes that, in a decentralised hydrogen architecture, the PEM electrolyser can be integrated directly on the wind turbine DC link. In other words, electrical power from the turbine is not exported as grid-quality AC and then rectified again for electrolysis. Instead, the turbine-side converter produces a controlled DC link, and the PEM stack or stack array is connected to that DC system.
The purpose of this section is to explain why that assumption is reasonable. The argument is made in two steps:

direct DC integration has a clear system-level benefit; and
direct DC integration without a separate electrolyser-side DC/DC converter is technically plausible if the turbine converter, PEM stack, and balance of plant are designed as one system.

The assumed electrical chain is:
wind generator -> active front-end rectifier / turbine-side converter -> controlled DC link -> PEM stack or PEM stack array
The turbine-side rectifier is assumed to be an active front end (AFE). It controls generator torque and DC-link voltage. The PEM stack or PEM stack array is connected directly to this controlled DC bus.
The architecture considered here has:

no AC grid export from the turbine;
no grid-side inverter;
no separate electrolyser-side DC/DC converter;
no dedicated stack current regulator between the DC link and the PEM stack;
only protection and auxiliary devices such as DC-link capacitance, contactors, precharge circuits, dump load, brake chopper, crowbar, and emergency shutdown hardware.







NoteModel assumption



The model assumes that direct DC integration is possible at system level. This does not imply that a PEM stack can simply be connected to an uncontrolled DC source. It assumes an integrated design in which the turbine AFE, DC-link control, PEM stack configuration, module switching, and balance-of-plant controls jointly keep the stack within its allowable operating envelope.

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
section: Why direct DC integration is beneficial
text:
Why direct DC integration is beneficial
The main benefit of direct DC integration is that it removes electrical conversion stages that are otherwise required only because the energy is temporarily handled as grid-quality AC.
In a conventional AC-coupled architecture, the electrical chain is typically:
wind generator -> machine-side converter -> DC link -> grid-side inverter -> transformer -> AC collection/export -> transformer/rectifier -> PEM stack
This architecture is logical when the product is electricity, or when many turbines feed a centralised electrolyser through an AC collection grid. However, if the only product is hydrogen and there is no AC export, several parts of the chain become avoidable from a system perspective.
A PEM electrolyser is fundamentally a DC load. A modern full-converter wind turbine also already contains a DC link internally. Direct integration therefore tries to connect two parts of the system that are already naturally DC:
wind turbine internal DC link -> PEM stack DC load
This creates four main benefits.

Fewer conversion stages
Each electrical conversion stage adds cost, losses, cooling demand, protection equipment, and controls. Direct DC integration removes the need to convert the turbine DC link back to grid-quality AC and then convert that AC back to DC for the electrolyser.
ITM Power’s wind-turbine electrolyser concept makes this argument explicitly. It describes a system with:

“an electrolyser placed at the wind turbine … directly electrically connected to the DC link in the wind turbine”

and states that Type IV turbine generators and their DC links:

“have the potential to power the electrolyser directly”

ITM describes the benefit as:

“fewer power conversion steps”

Source: ITM Power, Wind turbine electrolyser integration: https://itm-power.com/news/wind-turbine-electrolyser-integraion
This directly supports the modelling idea: if electricity is not the final export product, it is inefficient to force the system through the full AC-export chain before feeding a DC electrolyser.


Lower electrical infrastructure requirement
If hydrogen is produced at the turbine, the infield system can shift from electrical collection to hydrogen collection. This can reduce or avoid equipment such as turbine grid-side inverters, step-up transformers, offshore AC collection strings, offshore electrical platforms, and centralised electrolyser rectifier infrastructure.
The benefit is not only efficiency. It is also structural: the offshore wind farm no longer needs to be designed primarily as an electrical export asset. It can be designed as a distributed hydrogen-production asset.


Better fit with a decentralised hydrogen architecture
Direct DC integration aligns the electrical design with the value-chain design. In a decentralised architecture, each turbine becomes a hydrogen-production unit. The infield network collects hydrogen rather than electricity.
This avoids moving large amounts of electrical power through an infield AC grid only to convert it back to DC at a central electrolyser. It also reduces the relevance of AC string capacity limits and offshore electrical platform sizing in the model.


Potentially lower cost, mass, and losses
Removing conversion stages can reduce:

converter equipment;
transformers;
filters;
switchgear;
cooling systems;
electrical platform weight;
conversion losses;
interface complexity between turbine and central electrolyser.

These benefits are the reason direct DC integration is worth modelling, even if it requires a more integrated turbine-electrolyser control system.






TipModelling implication



The modelling assumption is not that power electronics disappear. The assumption is that the turbine-side AFE remains, while the downstream grid-side inverter and electrolyser-side rectification/DC conversion stages are not modelled as separate conversion steps for the decentralised case.
crumbs: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
section: Why direct DC integration should be technically possible
text:
Why direct DC integration should be technically possible
Direct DC integration should be technically possible because the two key electrical subsystems are compatible at a basic level:

a Type IV wind turbine already contains a controlled DC link; and
a PEM electrolyser stack is a DC electrochemical load.

The question is therefore not whether PEM electrolysis can use DC power. It can. The real question is whether the PEM stack can be operated safely and efficiently from the turbine DC link without adding a separate DC/DC converter between the DC link and the stack.
The answer is: yes, if the system is designed around voltage matching and active DC-link control.
crumbs: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
Electrical operating principle
A PEM electrolyser stack draws current according to its polarization curve. The stack voltage can be represented conceptually as:
V_stack = N_cells * V_cell(j, temperature, pressure, hydration, ageing)
where j is current density.
With a dedicated DC/DC converter, the converter normally regulates stack current. Without that converter, stack current is instead determined by the controlled DC-link voltage and the PEM polarization curve.
The direct-coupled design therefore becomes a voltage-matched system:
turbine DC-link operating range ~= PEM stack operating voltage range
If the PEM stack cell count and module configuration are selected so that the stack voltage range matches the turbine DC-link operating range, the stack can be supplied directly from the DC link. The AFE then acts as the main electrical actuator by regulating the DC-link voltage and controlling generator torque.
This is why the assumption is credible: the turbine-side AFE can provide controlled DC power, and the PEM stack can accept controlled DC power.

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
section: What replaces the missing DC/DC converter
crumbs: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
Evidence supporting the assumption

Industrial concept: ITM Power and Ørsted
ITM Power explicitly describes the direct DC-link integration concept. The proposed system places the electrolyser at the wind turbine and connects it directly to the turbine DC link:

“an electrolyser placed at the wind turbine … directly electrically connected to the DC link in the wind turbine”

ITM also states that Type IV turbine generators and their DC links:

“have the potential to power the electrolyser directly”

and that the approach enables:

“fewer power conversion steps”

Source: ITM Power, Wind turbine electrolyser integration: https://itm-power.com/news/wind-turbine-electrolyser-integraion
This source is important because it confirms that direct turbine-DC-link coupling is not merely a theoretical modelling shortcut. It is an identified industrial concept for reducing conversion stages in offshore wind-to-hydrogen systems.
However, ITM also states:

“Such a system has not been demonstrated in practice”

Therefore, this reference supports the feasibility and relevance of the model assumption, but it does not prove a commercial MW-scale no-DC/DC implementation.


Academic control concept: Jing et al.
Jing et al. describe a non-grid-connected wind-hydrogen architecture that:

“directly connects the Source wind turbines and the Load hydrogen production devices through a DC bus”

The electrolyser load is described as:

“a modular proton exchange membrane (PEM) water electrolytic hydrogen production array”

The control strategy regulates:

“the size of the PEM array … and DC voltage collaboratively”

Source: Jing et al., Research on the Structure and Control Strategy of Energy Router for Non-grid-connected Source-Load Hydrogen Production System: https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s42835-021-00774-w.pdf
This is directly aligned with the model assumption. Instead of using a separate DC/DC converter to regulate the PEM stack, the system controls the DC bus and changes the effective PEM array size. This supports the idea that a segmented stack array can replace part of the function of an electrolyser-side converter.
The limitation is that this is an academic control and simulation reference, not evidence of a commercial field system.


Small-scale experimental evidence: RMIT / Air 403
The RMIT direct-coupling work explicitly investigated wind-to-PEM coupling:

“without the need for intervening voltage converters and maximum power point tracking electronics”

Source: RMIT, Direct coupling of a fixed-pitch variable speed aerogenerator to PEM electrolysers in a wind-hydrogen system for remote area power supply: https://research-repository.rmit.edu.au/articles/conference_contribution/Direct_coupling_of_a_fixed-pitch_variable_speed_aerogenerator_to_PEM_electrolysers_in_a_wind-hydrogen_system_for_remote_area_power_supply/27369558
This supports the physical feasibility of direct wind-to-PEM coupling without voltage conversion. It is not directly scalable evidence for a modern MW offshore turbine, but it demonstrates that matching generator and PEM stack electrical characteristics can produce a working direct-coupled system.


Counterpoint: mainstream DC wind concepts often retain DC/DC
A recent paper on DC wind-turbine concepts notes that DC coupling is attractive because:

“wind turbine converters and electrolyzers operate internally with dc voltage”

and that DC concepts can reduce equipment because:

“the number of conversion stages and filters is reduced”

However, the same paper states:

“A key component for the application of dc technology in WTs is a high-power dc-dc converter”

with functions including:

“stepping up/down dc voltages, galvanic isolation and fault ride-through capability”

Source: Evaluation of wind turbine concepts for coupling wind energy and electrolysis: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10010-025-00796-0
This is an important qualification. It shows that direct DC integration is attractive, but a separate DC/DC converter is still often used in mainstream DC architectures because it provides voltage adaptation, isolation, and protection. Therefore, the no-DC/DC assumption is technically defensible but should be treated as an integrated-design assumption rather than a standard off-the-shelf configuration.

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
Technical conditions for feasibility

Voltage matching
The PEM stack voltage range must match the turbine DC-link voltage range. This can be done by selecting the number of cells in series and by arranging stack modules in suitable series/parallel strings.
If the DC-link voltage is too low, the stack will produce little or no hydrogen. If it is too high, current density can exceed stack limits. The design therefore needs voltage matching at rated power and acceptable operation across the expected wind-power range.


Current-density control
The PEM stack must remain within its allowable current-density range. A review of dynamic PEM operation notes:

“Dynamic operation of PEM electrolyzers via modulation of the operating current density brings with it several challenges - namely, heat management, gas crossover and associated safety concerns, and stack degradation.”

Source: https://arxiv.org/html/2405.06766v1
In the direct DC architecture, current density is constrained by DC-link voltage control, wind curtailment, and stack-module switching rather than by a dedicated DC/DC converter.


Low-load and gas-crossover limits
Low-load operation can create gas-purity and crossover concerns. A direct wind-coupled system will naturally encounter low-load operation unless it is curtailed, idled, or shut down below a defined threshold.
A 2025 RSC paper on PEM operation at differential pressure states that understanding hydrogen permeation is:

“critical towards developing effective gas recombination strategies that enable safe operation”

Source: https://pubs.rsc.org/en/Content/ArticleLanding/2025/EE/D5EE00048C
The model assumption should therefore include a minimum safe operating region or shutdown logic. Direct DC connection should not be interpreted as uncontrolled low-current operation at arbitrary pressure.


Dynamic response
PEM electrolysers are suitable for dynamic renewable operation, but dynamic operation still affects degradation, thermal control, pressure control, and gas management. A review of PEM dynamic operation states that renewable intermittency:

“poses several technological challenges and financial questions”

Source: https://www.mdpi.com/1996-1073/18/9/2154
This does not invalidate direct DC integration. It means the stack and balance of plant must be qualified for turbine-driven operation, including ramps, starts, stops, and curtailment events.


Thermal and water management
At high current density, the stack generates more heat and requires adequate water supply and cooling. In a direct DC architecture, the AFE and the PEM balance of plant must coordinate closely so the stack is only energised when water flow, temperature, pressure, and conductivity are within safe limits.


Fault handling
The most critical event is a PEM stack trip while the turbine is producing power. Without a DC/DC converter, there is less electrical decoupling between the turbine and the stack. The DC link can overvoltage quickly unless excess energy is removed or turbine power is reduced.
A credible direct DC architecture therefore needs:

fast torque reduction by the AFE;
pitch or aerodynamic curtailment;
DC brake chopper or dump load;
high-speed stack isolation;
controlled DC-link discharge;
safe hydrogen shutdown sequence.

title: Direct DC Integration of Wind Turbines and PEM Electrolysers
text:
Final assessment
Direct integration of a PEM stack or PEM stack array on the turbine DC link is a reasonable model assumption for a decentralised hydrogen architecture.
The main reason to use it is that it avoids unnecessary AC conversion when the final product is hydrogen rather than electricity. It can reduce conversion stages, electrical infrastructure, equipment mass, and conversion losses. It also fits the system logic of a decentralised hydrogen wind farm, where the infield network collects hydrogen instead of electricity.
Technically, the assumption is defensible because a PEM stack is a DC load and a Type IV wind turbine already has a controlled DC link. With an AFE controlling DC-link voltage and generator torque, and with a voltage-matched segmented PEM stack array, direct operation without a separate electrolyser-side DC/DC converter should be possible.
The strongest practical formulation is:
AFE-controlled turbine DC link + voltage-matched segmented PEM stack array + module switching + emergency dump/brake protection






ImportantFeasibility statement



The model assumption is credible: direct DC integration without a separate electrolyser-side DC/DC converter should be technically possible, provided the turbine AFE, PEM stack array, protection system, and balance of plant are co-designed. It is not yet a widely proven MW-scale commercial standard, so it should be treated as an advanced integrated architecture rather than an off-the-shelf configuration.
